coredata.dc:title: B-doped graphene quantum dots implanted into bimetallic organic framework as a highly active and robust cathodic catalyst in the microbial fuel cell
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Yimin
Hou, Yanping
Yu, Zebin
Tu, Lingli
Qin, Shanming
Lan, Danquan
Chen, Shuo
Sun, Jiangli
Wang, Shuangfei
coredata.dc:description: Developing efficient and durable oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) cathodic catalysts plays an essential role in application of microbial fuel cells (MFCs). Herein, the B-doped graphene quantum dots implanted into bimetallic organic framework (BGQDs/MOF-t) are fabricated by a facile electro-deposition. Results show that, the in-situ growth of FeCoMOF on nickel foam can effectively assist construction of nanoflowers with compact connections, thus improves the conductivity. More importantly, this nano-network can serve as the template for the implantation of BGQDs through powerful interface of M-O-C bonding, avoiding π-π rearrangement and providing efficient charge transfer and abundant edge active sites. Benefitting from the enhanced electrode/electrolyte transport interface, abundant catalytic sites and low charge transfer resistance, the BGQDs/MOF-15 exhibits excellent ORR activity, superior to commercial Pt/C catalyst. In the MFC with the BGQDs/MOF-15 cathode, the maximum power density of 703.55 mW m−2 is achieved, which is 1.53 times of that of the Pt/C cathode. In addition, the BGQDs/MOF-15 cathode maintains great stability over 800 h, while that of Pt/C reduces to 61% of the initial voltage. This work opens new opportunities for developing efficient and durable MOF-derived ORR catalyst.

head.ce:title: B-doped graphene quantum dots implanted into bimetallic organic framework as a highly active and robust cathodic catalyst in the microbial fuel cell

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing efficient and durable oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) cathodic catalysts plays an essential role in application of microbial fuel cells (MFCs). Herein, the B-doped graphene quantum dots implanted into bimetallic organic framework (BGQDs/MOF-t) are fabricated by a facile electro-deposition. Results show that, the in-situ growth of FeCoMOF on nickel foam can effectively assist construction of nanoflowers with compact connections, thus improves the conductivity. More importantly, this nano-network can serve as the template for the implantation of BGQDs through powerful interface of M-O-C bonding, avoiding π-π rearrangement and providing efficient charge transfer and abundant edge active sites. Benefitting from the enhanced electrode/electrolyte transport interface, abundant catalytic sites and low charge transfer resistance, the BGQDs/MOF-15 exhibits excellent ORR activity, superior to commercial Pt/C catalyst. In the MFC with the BGQDs/MOF-15 cathode, the maximum power density of 703.55 mW m−2 is achieved, which is 1.53 times of that of the Pt/C cathode. In addition, the BGQDs/MOF-15 cathode maintains great stability over 800 h, while that of Pt/C reduces to 61% of the initial voltage. This work opens new opportunities for developing efficient and durable MOF-derived ORR catalyst.

ce:section.ce:para: Water scarcity and energy crisis are two huge challenges that our society are facing currently. Among the efficient, durable energy generation/conversion devices, microbial fuel cells (MFCs) are emerging as a promising technology due to their long cycle life and environmental friendliness (Zhang et al., 2011, 2018a). In a typical MFC, electrochemically active microorganisms on the anode oxidize biodegradable organic matters into CO2, electrons and protons, whilst electrons and protons are transferred to the cathode to participate in oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), so as to complete the conversion of chemical energy into renewable electricity (Mahalingam et al., 2021). Nevertheless, the high overpotential and sluggish reaction kinetics of ORR highly limit MFCs development and practical application (Ayyaru et al., 2019; Mahalingam et al., 2018). Although Pt-based catalysts have been demonstrated to be efficient ORR catalysts, they are not suitable for commercial spread due to their poor stability, scarcity and prohibitive cost (Zhang et al., 2014; Yang et al., 2019a). As a result, a wide variety of alternatives have been recently proposed, ranging from transition metal catalysts (e.g. Cu–Sn, oxide/bismuth tungstate, and sulfides) (Noori et al., 2018; Das et al., 2018), carbon-based catalysts (e.g. carbon nanofibers/nanotubes and phthalocyanine-derived carbon), biocatalysts, to newly developed metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and MOF derivatives (Yuan et al., 2016; Murata et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2019b; Noori and Verma, 2019).

Among these catalysts, MOFs exhibit broad potential because of their high crystallinity, tunable pore structures and high specific surface area (Rego et al., Kigga; Bennett and Cheetham, 2014). Also, MOFs derivatives can inherit the size, morphology, structure and other characteristics of MOFs, hence, showing great potential as the electrocatalysts (Das et al., 2020). However, for MOFs, the metal sites are usually blocked by the organic linkers, which is not conducive to reactions (Chen et al., 2020). To address this issue, bimetallic MOFs were developed by incorporating secondly metal ions to the nodes. The partial substitution of secondary metal ions in MOFs would allow exposing more active sites and showing synergistic effect for electrocatalysis (Masoomi et al., 2019; Rice et al., 2019; Abednatanzi et al., 2019; Yang and Xu, 2017). CuNi/C derived from bimetallic MOF showed better ORR catalytic performance than monometallic counterparts in terms of limiting current density, onset potential and half-wave potential, and better stability than Pt -based materials (Wu et al., 2017). This was mainly due to the synergistic effect of Cu and Ni, as well as the enhancement of carbon matrix. Moreover, the exposed metal atoms in MOFs derivatives could improve the electrical conductivity and generate more active sites (Lu et al., 2019). Xu et al. prepared two-dimensional (2D) NiCo MOF@ZIF-L (Zn)@ZIF-67, which displayed high ORR activity comparable to Pt/C (E1/2 = 0.866 V; Eonset = 1.038 V) (Xu et al., 2019a).

However, during the application in fuel cells, the powdered MOFs were usually loaded onto substrate materilas using binders (such as Nafion), which increased the contact resistance and causes the coverage of some active sites (Guan et al., 2017; He et al., 2019). In situ growth of weakly conductive catalysts on nickel foam (NF) can effectively improve charge transfer rate and strengthen the contact between catalysts and substrate materials (Raja et al., 2018; Senthil Raja et al., 2018). Liang et al. demonstrated that NiO/Ni electrode exhibited excellent electrocatalytic performance comparable to those of IrO2 and RuO2 electrodes, which was mainly attributed to the well connection of 3D NF and the intact contact between NiO and NF, providing excellent transport network (Liang et al., 2016). In this regard, we have recently reported the MOF-derived FexCo3-xS4/FeyCo9-yS8 in-situ grown on NF as efficient ORR catalyst with high doping of N and S (Yan et al., 2020). On the role of FeCoMOF, the FeCoS(MOF) microspheres were made up of nanosheets piling-up without phase aggregation and structural collapse, ensuring a large contact area with O2 and exposing more active sites. Nevertheless, due to the long subsequent reaction (heteroatomic doping), the mechanical strength decreased significantly. As a result, it is commendably desired to develop MOF-based catalysts with higher electrocatalytic performance via simple strategies.

Bimetallic MOFs with the combination of functional materials could enhance electrical conductivity, chemical stability and mechanical strength of MOFs, which has been used for electric catalytic application. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a quasi 0 D carbon material, have more abundant active sites (edge defects, functional groups, doping, etc.) than 2D graphene (Fan et al., 2019). Liang et al. (Huang and Liao, 2019) proposed a simple hydrothermal method for preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots on the surface of nitrogen-doped carbon nanotubes (CQDs/N- CNTs), which showed low onset potential (- 0.03 V vs. Ag/AgCl) and high limiting diffusion current (~5.5 mA cm−2); Tam et al. synthesized boron-doped graphene quantum dots (BGQDs) at low temperature by one-step method using glucose as a precursor, and compounded with reduced graphene oxide (RGO) as metal free electrocatalyst (G-BGQD) for ORR (Van Tam et al., 2017). The G-BGQD showed excellent onset potential and limiting current density comparable to that of Pt/C, in which BGQD provided abundant active sites for ORR. Previous studies have also shown that incorporating GQDs with metal oxides could effectively improve ORR performance (Shaari et al., 2020). Undoubtedly, considerable attention should be devoted to GQDs because of their rich active sites, fast electron transfer, and improved electrical conductivity. Yu-Chuan Chen et al. incorporated GQDs into a zirconium-based MOF by direct impregnation, which resulted in two orders of enhancement of electrical conductivity (Chen et al., 2019). However, the synergistic effect between GQDs and MOF remains to be further explored.

On the one hand, the FeCoMOF template has a high specific surface area, which can guide the assembly of BGQDs, effectively inhibit the aggregation of BGQDs, and improve the stability; At the same time, BGQDs binding exposes more active sites and promotes charge transfer and diffusion. On the other hand, the formation of M-O-C covalent bond improves the conductivity and stability. The deterioration of mechanical properties can be avoided by electrodeposition.

Based on the above discussion, boron doped graphene quantum dots (BGQDs) implanted in FeCoMOF (BGQDs/MOF-t) catalyst was synthesized via electrodeposition (Fig. 1 a.). Various characterizations were performed to analyze and confirm the successful doping and surface chemical formation of BGQDs/MOF-t. Electrochemical tests were further conducted to determine ORR catalytic activity of BGQDs/MOF-t and the structure-activity relationship of the catalysts. The resultant BCQDs/MOF catalyst exhibited excellent ORR performance, even outperforming Pt/C catalyst, and improved stability by 58.6% relative to Pt/C. When being applied to a double-chamber MFC, the maximum power density of BGQDs/MOF was 1.53 times of that of Pt/C electrode.

Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O), ferric trichloride tetrahydrate (FeCl3·4H2O), ammonium fluoride (NH4F), urea (CO(NH2)2), dimethylformamide (DMF), anhydrous ethanol (C2H6O), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), citric acid (C6H8O7), ammonis water (NH3·H2O), boric acid (H3BO3) and isopropyl alcohol ((CH3)2CHOH) were purchased from Guangdong Guanghua Sci-tech Co., Ltd. The 2,5-diaminoterephthalic acid (NH2-BDC) and phenylboronic acid (C6H7BO2) were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Pt/C catalyst was bought from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. All the chemicals were reagent grade and used as received.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of BGQDs/MOF-t and Pt/C electrode
ce:section.ce:para:
The BGQDs/MOF-t in-situ grown on NF was prepared by a facile and effective method. The illustration of the key strategy involved in the synthetic procedure for BGQDs/MOF-t electrode was displayed in Fig. 1a. Firstly, the FeCoMOF grown on NF and BGQDs solution were synthesized by solvothermal method respectively. The obtained FeCoMOF with large specific surface area was served as the template to direct the assembly of BGQDs. Subsequently, the simple electrophoretic deposition process was adopted for deposition and implantation of BGQDs on FeCoMOF nanosheets surface. Various deposition times (5 min, 15 min, 20 min) were conducted to investigate the effects of deposition times on morphology and physicochemical properties of materials.
(1)
Synthesis of FeCoMOF in-situ grown on NF:

The optimized preparation of the FeCoMOF was based on our previous study (Yan et al., 2020). In short, the precursor was grown in-situ on the NF firstly. Five millimole Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 1.0 mmol FeCl3·4H2O, 2.0 mmol NH4F and 5.0 mmol urea were dissolved in 30 mL of isopropyl alcohol solution (Vdeionized water:V isopropyl alcohol = 1:1). The pure NF was immersed in the growth solution, and then transferred into the stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 140 °C for 7 h. Subsequently, the growth solution was prepared by dissolving 400 mg of NH2-BDC in 80 mL DMF. A piece of the pre-treated NF was added to the above solution and the mixed solution was transferred into a 100-mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, treated at 160 °C for 12 h in an electric oven. The as-prepared material was denoted as FeCoMOF.
(2)
Synthesis of B-doped graphene quantum dots (BGQDs):

Citric acid (1.00 g) was dissolved in NH3·H2O (10 mL) solution, denoted as solution A. Phenylboronic acid (0.10 g) and boric acid (0.06 g) were dissolved in a solution containing isopropyl alcohol (20 mL) ethanol (5 mL), denoted as solution B. After intense sonication of solution A for 30 min, solution B was slowly added into solution A, and stirred for 15 min. The mixed solution was transferred into the stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 205 °C for 24 h. The BGQDs were collected by filtering the final solution using microporous PTFE membrane (50 mm, 0.22 μm). For comparison, GQDs samples were prepared under similar conditions without addition of phenylboronic acid and boric acid.
(3)
Synthesis of BGQDs/MOF-t:

Electrophoretic deposition (EPD) was carried out to implant BGQDs into FeCoMOF. The counter electrode and working electrode were graphite rod and FeCoMOF, respectively, which were immersed into the aqueous solution of BGQDs at constant potential of −6.0 V. By varying the deposition time, the BGQDs with controllable mass loading could be achieved. The obtained materials were labeled as BGQDs/MOF-t (t means deposition time, 0–20 min). The calculations of mass loading were provided in Table S1.
(4)
Preparation of Pt/C electrode (0.3 mg cm−2):

The Pt/C catalyst was dispersed in the mixture of Nafion (20 μL) and isopropanol (1 mL) via ultrasonication for 30 min; then, the Pt/C catalyst ink was coated onto the NF (cross sectional area of 7.065 cm−2) and dried overnight.

The crystal structures of the catalysts were identified by high power (4 kW)X-ray diffractometer XRD (XRD; DX2700A, Dandong Haoyuan Instrument Co., Ltd,China) equipped with a Cu sealed tube at a scanning rate of 8° min−1 within the 2θ range of 5°–90°. Surface chemical compositions of the electrocatalysts were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo fisher Scientific K-Alpha) equipped with an Al (single) X-ray source and calibrated against a variable carbon (binding energy = 284.8 eV) signal binding energy. Morphologies and microstructures of as-synthesized ORR catalysts were obtained by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi SU8220, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 5 kV and by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM 2100). The chemical bonds of the catalysts were characterized by Fourier Infrared Spectrometer (IRTracer-100, Shimadzu, Japan), using KBr as the diluent.

Electrochemical tests were performed on the electrochemical workstation (CHI 660E, CH Instruments Chenhua Corp.,China) using standard three-electrode cell saturated with O2. In this system, the as-prepared electrodes and graphite rod were served as the working electrode and counter electrode respectively, and the saturated calomel electrode (SCE) was adopted as the reference electrode. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was carried out within the potential range of 0.2 V to −1.0 V with a same scan rate of 5 mV s−1. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed at an open circuit potential (OCP) in the frequency range of 100 kHz to 10 mHz, and the charge transfer resistance (Rct) was obtained by Zview 3.10 software (Echem, US). Tafel curve was recorded via sweeping in the overpotential range (η) of 0–100 mV with a scan rate of 5 mV s−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MFC setup, operation, and performance examination
ce:section.ce:para: The two-chamber MFC was made from plexiglass and with an internal diameter of 3.0 cm and a length of 4.0 cm for each chamber (effective volume was 28 mL). The carbon brush anode was fabricated using graphite fiber, and the above prepared electrodes, as well as the Pt/C electrode (0.3 mg cm−2) were used directly as cathodes (cross sectional area of 7.065 cm−2), respectively. The anode and cathode were connected using titanium wire, with an external resistor of 1000 Ω between the electrodes, and the two chambers were separated by a proton exchange membrane. Firstly, the MFCs were inoculated with domestic wastewater collected from Nanning Langdong Sewage Treatment Plant. After the output electricity stabilized during start-up, the domestic wastewater was replaced regularly with fresh media. The media consisted of 50 mM phosphate buffer with the followings: 1.0 g L−1 sodium acetate, mineral solution (5.0 ml L−1), and vitamin solution (0.2 ml L−1). Perallelled MFCs were operated in fed batch mode at room temperature, and the medium was replaced when the output voltage dropped to lower than 0.1 V.

The SEM, EDS and TEM images of pure FeCoMOF nanosheets and BGQDs/MOF-15 electrode were shown in Fig. 1 and Fig. S1. As shown in Fig. 1b, numerous FeCoMOF were densely distributed throughout the NF substrate. Compared with the rod-like morphology of powdered MIL-88B and its analogs in other literature, all the nanosheets were closely interconnected with each other to form the flower-like morphology, which could facilitate rapid electrons trasfer and mass transfer (Yi et al., 2020; Tran et al., 2020; Liao et al., 2020). Furthermore, in-situ grown of catalyst on NF without any adhesive could avoid the coverage of active sites and facilitate reduction of internal resistance. Similarly, this 3D structure were also tightly attached on the NF in BGQDs/MOF-t electrode materials, as shown in Fig. 1c and Fig. S1. These images demonstrated that the deposition of BGQDs on FeCoMOF caused no significant morphology change and negligible element agglomeration (Fig. 1d–i). In agreement with SEM results, nanosheets also can be observed in FeCoMOF and BGQDs/MOF-15 separated from NF in the TEM images (Fig. 1j and k). However, quantum dots cannot be clearly observed at this scale because of their small size (lateral dimensions <10 nm, thickness <2 nm) (Wang et al., 2016). The TEM image of BGQDs solution showed that the sizes of the BGQDs were in the range of 1–4 nm with an average size of 2.69 nm (Fig. 1).

To further corroborate the detailed structural features of as-synthesized BGQDs/MOF-t, HRTEM analysis was carried out, as depicted in Fig. 2 . Many dots were evenly distributed in the field without agglomeration, indicating that a large amount of BGQDs were in situ formed on the surfaces of the FeCoMOF through EPD process (Fig. 2a and b). As revealed in Fig. 2c, the clear lattice spacing of 0.2418 nm corresponded to the (1120) plane of graphite structure, suggesting the high crystalline structure of the BGQDs in BGQDs/MOF-15 (Van Tam et al., 2017; Tam et al., 2019). As shown in Fig. S2, the UV–vis absorption of BGQDs/MOF showed two clear bands at 240 and 360 nm, which were attributed to the π–π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains and the characteristic absorption peak of GQDs (n–π* transition) (Fan et al., 2019; Zheng et al., 2014; Samantara et al., 2015). These unique optical properties were typical features of GQDs, as previously reported. These characteristics indicated that BGQDs were successfully synthesized and uniformly implanted into the FeCoMOF. In the as-described architecture, FeCoMOF nanosheets with high surface area allowed facile transportation of electrolytes and electrical reactants/products, as well as exposure of more active sites. Meanwhile, the existence of BGQDs were highly beneficial for ORR by offering abundant edge active sites and oxygen-containing functional groups, which were potential active sites for ORR (Yan et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2019b). What's more, in the process of EPD, BGQDs were uniformly and firmly implanted into FeCoMOF, which would greatly enhance the electrocatalytic activity and stability of the catalyst, attributing to the synergistic effect of BGQDs and FeCoMOF, as well as the mechanical interlocking and covalent bonding between FeCoMOF and BGQDs. Besides, as can be seen from Fig. 2d and e, the sizes of BGQDs ranged from 1 nm to 4 nm, indicating that BGQDs would agglomerate after 15 min of deposition, causing the reduction of effective active sites.

Fig. 3 a shows the XRD patterns of FeCoMOF, BGQDs/MOF–t and BGQDs/NF-15. The diffraction peaks of all samples agreed with the XRD patterns of Fe-MIL-88B–NH2 and MIL-88B, except for three highly crystalline peaks of NF at 44.9°, 52.2°and 76.7° (PDF no.04-0850) (Yan et al., 2020; Bauer et al., 2008; Wei et al., 2015). Further, the diffraction peaks of carbon materials were hardly observed after EPD, which was probably due to the ultralow mass loading of BGQDs and overlap with other strong peaks. To avoid the interference of FeCoMOF, BGQDs loaded on NF by electrodeposition was used for XRD test. It could be found that a weak peak appeared at 22.5° corresponded to graphite (Liang et al., 2020). It was calculated to be ~1.951 nm by Scherrer formula in the diffraction peaks, consistent with HRTEM statistical result.

The Raman spectrum was performed to identify carbon materials in the as-prepared electrodes. As displayed in Fig. 3b, upshifts in D (1350 cm−1), D〞(1417 cm−1) and G bands (at 1595 cm−1) were observed when the QDs were in situ grown on the surface of FeCoMOF, implying the existence of carbon materials. Combined with the TEM and XRD results, it could be concluded that the carbon materials were BGQDs. The D band was ascribed to disorder or defects that existed in carbon lattice, and the D〞related to amorphous sp2 phase band, while G band arose due to sp2 orbital of in-plane stretching vibration (Gupta et al., 2016; Alam et al., 2020). The intensity ratio of ID/IG is generally an effective indicator to reflect the degree of defect/disorder degree in carbon materials (Tang et al., 2019). It should be noted that the longer the electrodeposition time, the higher the ID/IG value would be, implying a higher proportion of defects (e.g. vacancies and introduction of B atoms in carbon skeleton) (Jia et al., 2018a). Doping of electron-deficient B atoms could be beneficial to ORR because they would increase the hole-type charge carriers, reduce the free energy of the reaction and facilitate an effective chemisorption with the negatively charged oxygen atoms (Tam et al., 2019; Yu et al., 2018).

XPS and FTIR were then further employed to verify materials’ surface chemical compositions, their binding states as well as the functional groups. Basically, as shown in FT-IR spectra (Fig. 4 a), the FeCoMOF and BGQDs/MOF-t appeared similar stretching vibrations before and after EPD. However, the spectra of BGQDs/MOF-t also exhibited additional absorption peaks. The B–C stretching at 1019 cm−1 proved that some carbon atoms of graphene were substituted by boron atoms (Spitler and Dichtel, 2010). Also 1043 and 1090 cm−1 were the characteristic absorption peaks of the M-O-C bond, indicating that BGQDs and MOF could be connected through covalent bond, which ensured the compact packing, efficient charge transfer and stable structure.

The XPS was used to demonstrate the surface elemental characteristics. The high-resolution spectra were shown in Fig. 4b–(e). The C 1s spectra (Fig. 4b) were all deconvoluted into three main peaks ascribed to the C–C (sp3)/CC (sp2) (284.7 eV), C–O (286.0 eV), O–CO (288.7 eV), respectively (Wang et al., 2020). Besides, compared to FeCoMOF, BGQDs/MOF possessed a lower content of C and N, which was mainly due to the hydrogen evolution reaction on NF substrate during electrodeposition, leading to some loss of catalyst (Table S2). The O 1s peaks at 532.1 eV, 532.5 eV, and 532.8 eV were associated with low oxygen defects, CO quinone-type groups, and C–O ether groups, respectively. Notably, apart from these bonds, an unusual peak at 530.3 eV appeared in BGQD/MOF-t, confirming the formation of M-O-C, and the M-O-C was generally considered to be the bonding of BGQDs and FeCoMOF (Jia et al., 2018b). The B 1s peaks at 189.1 eV, 190.2 eV, 191.2 eV and 192.4 eV were attributed to graphitic-like BC3, BC2O, BCO2 and B–O structures, respectively (Van Tam et al., 2017; Tam et al., 2019). As shown in Table S2, with the increase of deposition time, the corresponding boron atomic percentages (derived from XPS elemental quantitative analysis) increased, accounting for 7.14 at %, 8.65 at % and 11.21 at % for BGQDs/MOF-5, BGQDs/MOF-15 and BGQDs/MOF-20, respectively; which was consistent with the results of Raman. The N 1s could be fitted into two peaks with one peak at 399.7 eV for pyrrolic-N and another at 401.0 eV for graphitic-N. The highly active N species may promote the oxygen reduction reaction (Zhao et al., 2020).

Furthermore, the ratio of the area under the deconvolated curve for each element was used for preliminary quantitative analysis of chemical bonds (Fig. 4d and Table S3). In summary: (1) The contents of B element and graphite-like BC3 in BGQDs/MOF-t increased with the prolonging of deposition time. The low electronegativity of boron atom and BC3 would result in charge redistribution in carbon plane, accelerating electron transfer, improving electronic conductivity, oxygen chemisorption, and creating polarized sites as ORR active catalytic sites in BGQDs (Van Tam et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2019). (2) The content of C–O bonds increased after electrodeposition, which might be owing to the introduction of oxygen-containing functional groups by quantum dots. Besides, it was speculated that the M-O-C, a strong covalent bond connecting FeCoMOF and BGQD, was conducive to electron transfer (Lu et al., 2014). The conjecture was confirmed in high-resolution O1s spectrum. The content of M-O-C at 530.3 eV changed significantly with different deposition time: When the deposition time was prolonged to 15 min, the content of M-O-C gradually increased from 0 to 12.62%; however, when the deposition time was extended to 20 min, the content of M-O-C tended to be stable (12.89%), indicating that the electrodeposition time played an important role in the formation of M-O-C, and the interface structure between FeCoMOF and BGQDs changed with time. (3) According to the previous characterization, it could be inferred that within 0–15 min, the dominant connection mode between MOF and BGQDs was covalent bond M-O-C; then, with the extension of deposition time, the M-O-C bonds gradually saturated, and the exterior clustered BGQDs began to grow on the edge of BGQDs without the formation of M-O-C, resulting in larger particle size and fuzzy boundary.

The electrochemical activities of various cathode catalysts were evaluated by LSV, EIS, and Tafel plots, as shown in Fig. 5 and Fig. S3. Generally, LSV curve was a useful tool to analyze ORR activity because it was directly proportional to the current density (Kumar et al., 2018). According to Fig. S3a, it can be concluded that the optimal concentration of BGQDs solution for BGQDs/MOF-t was 2.1 mg mL−1. Since the regulation of BGQDs concentration and deposition time both reflected the influence of BGQDs content on the catalyst performance, the discussion of ORR activities with various deposition times were therefore conducted (the default concentration of BGQDs solution is 2.1 mg mL−1).

In Fig. 5a, BGQDs/MOF-15 ecxhibited the highest current density among different cathode materials. For instance, at −0.10 V, the current densities of different catalysts followed the order: BGQDs/MOF-15＞Pt/C＞BGQDs/MOF-20＞BGQDs/MOF-10＞FeCoMOF＞ BGQDs/MOF-5＞NF. The maximum current density of −0.0601 A cm−2 achieved from the BGQDs/MOF-15 cathode at the potential of −0.10 V, which was 1.17 times that of the Pt/C catalyst (−0.0510 A cm−2) and 1.77 times that of FeCoMOF catalyst. Onset potential is a key index of ORR, higher onset potential implies better catalytic properties (Xue et al., 2019). As listed in Table S4, BGQDs/MOF-15 exhibited more positive onset potential (0.014 V) than those of other materials and closed to Pt/C catalyst (0.04 V). Both the enhanced current density and positive onset potential showed by BGQDs/MOF-15 indicated that the implantation of BGQDs into FeCoMOF contributed to the outstanding ORR activity, and the optimal electrodeposition time was 15 min.

Similar trends were also observed on EIS fitting results. After the implantation of BGQDs, a significant decrease in diameter of the semicircle arc can be seen in Fig. 5b. According to the related data in Table S4, BGQDs/MOF-15 showed lower charge transfer resistance (Rct,7.536 Ω) compared to FeCoMOF (10.84 Ω). The Rct followed the following order: Pt/C (4.180Ω) ＜BGQDs/MOF-15 (7.536Ω) ＜BGQDs/MOF-20 (10.84 Ω) ＜ BGQDs/MOF-10 (12.69 Ω) ＜FeCoMOF (12.92 Ω) ＜BGQDs/MOF-5 (16.91 Ω). These results evidenced that the FeCoMOF electrodes modified by quantum dots were more conducive to electron transfer, which could lead to higher rate of oxygen reduction reaction (Kumar et al., 2018).

The exchange current density (i 0), as an important parameter to study the catalysis kinetics of materials, is signed through the Tafel curve in Fig. 5c (Zhang et al., 2018b). The results of linear fitting were listed in Table S4 (R2 > 0.99). The i 0 of BGQD/MOF-15 was 29.59 × 10−4 A cm−2, remarkably greater than that of bare NF (0.05711 × 10−4 A cm−2), and similar to that of Pt/C (36.26 × 10−4 A cm−2). The i 0 of the BGQDs/MOF-t were all larger than NF (0.05711 × 10−4 A cm−2) and FeCoMOF (0.2008 × 10−4 A cm−2). It was believed that the larger i 0 stood for more effective utilization of electrons and was beneficial for ORR kinetics (Zhang et al., 2018b). Thus, the trend of i 0 further verified that the implantion of the BGQDs promoted the electrochemical activity and kinetics of the cathodes, which were in accordance with LSV and EIS results.

Subsequently, the electrochemically active surface areas (ECSA) confirmed similar results (Table S4). As known, the ECSA could be well used to evaluate the activation sites of materials (He et al., 2020). As ECSA is proportional to electrochemical double-layer capacitance (Cdl), the CV in a non-Faradaic region was used to obtain the Cdl and ECSA (Fig. S4) (Jiang et al., 2018). Apparently, in Fig. S4, the calculated ECSAs increased in the order of BGQDs/MOF-5 (74.57 cmECSA 2)＜ FeCoMOF (85.71 cmECSA 2) ＜BGQDs/MOF-20 (86.57 cmECSA 2)＜BGQDs/MOF-10 (124.29 cmECSA 2)＜Pt/C (142.86 cmECSA 2)＜BGQDs/MOF-15 (181.43 cmECSA 2). Thus, the optimal proportion of BGQD implanted in MOF was verified to be 6.98 mg cm−2 in this study (Table S1). As can be seen from Fig. 5d, under the optimal proportion, its electrocatalytic parameters were close to or even better than those of Pt/C, and greatly outperformed the FeCoMOF.

In order to illustrate the role of boron in ORR, GQDs/MOF-15 was prepared and electrochemical testing was performed. As shown in Fig. S5, boron dopants improved the electrocatalytic activity of the material and reduced Rct. This could be attributed to that electron-deficient B could modify electronic structure of nanocarbons (Mahalingam et al., 2020). The graphite-like BC3 structure has favorable oxygen adsorption capacity and excellent electron transport feature, which is conducive to ORR (Yu et al., 2018).

The abovementioned results proved the superiority of BGQDs/MOF-15 for ORR. The excellent performance can be chiefly assigned to advantageous structural features and chemical compositions. Particularly, MOF enhanced the surface area, which provided more electroactive sites. Meanwhile, the in situ growth of MOF in NF effectively improved the conductivity and chemical activity. The doping of boron and the formation of BC3 structure could effectively reduce the charge transfer resistance and improve the electrical conductivity. Moreover, the 0 D structure of BGQDs could reduce the charge transport lengths, and the abundant marginal active sites greatly enhanced the ORR catalytic activity.

The Fe 2p and Co 2p spectra of FeCoMOF and BGQDs/MOF-15 were shown in Fig. S5. The energy spectra of Fe and Co shifted to a lower binding energy when BGQDs were implanted into FeCoMOF, due to the strong interaction between them. This synergistic effect could promote charge transfer between surface cations and adsorbents, thus promoting ORR kinetics (Li et al., 2019).

To gain further insight into the ORR catalytic mechanisms of BGQDs and FeCoMOF, BGQDs/NF-t were prepared as comparison samples for electrochemical measurement. As displayed in Fig. S3b and Fig. 6 a, for various BGQDs/MOF-t materials, the current density at −1.0 V gradually increased with the extension of time from 5 min to 15 min; and by prolonging the deposition time to 20 min, although the catalytic performance was improved compared with the original MOF, it greatly deteriorated compared with BGQDs/MOF-15. Therefore, there was a well balance between the BGQDs and the FeCoMOF. The current of BGQDs/NF-t increased with time and decreased only slightly after 15 min. Similar phenomena occurred in Rct, ECSA, and i0 tests (Fig. 6, Fig. S3 and Fig. S4).

Possible explanation for the above results could be as follows: Briefly, within 0–5 min, under the strong hydrogen evolution reaction of NF, part of the precipitate catalyst fall off, resulting in the decline of ORR performance. When the deposition time ranged from 5 min to 15 min, BGQDs were gradually connected to MOF which were stably attached to NF by covalent M-O-C bonding, improving ORR performance (Ali et al., 2021). Furthermore, when prolonging the deposition time to 20 min, BGQDs could only continue to grow at the edge of GQDs, resulting in serious agglomeration and reduced activity. However, for BGQDs/NF-t, the main connection bonding mode was π – π stacking, thus, the performance was slightly decreased due to agglomeration after 15 min, which demonstrated the important role of covalent M-O-C bonding in improving the ORR performance of BGQDs/MOF-t.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MFCs performances using the as-prepared cathodes
ce:section.ce:para: To further assess the catalytic activities of the BGQDs/MOF-t cathodes in MFCs, power density, polarization curves and cell voltage curve were determined. Pt/C cathode was used as the benchmark. As presented in Fig. 7 a, the exhibited maximum power densities (MPD) sequences of the MFCs were: BGQDs/MOF-15 > Pt/C > BGQDs/MOF-20 > BGQDs/MOF-10 > FeCoMOF > BGQDs/MOF-5. The MPD from the MFC with the BGQDs/MOF-15 cathode (704.24 mW m−2) was 1.53 times of the MFC with Pt/C (460.29 mW m−2), and 2.4 times of that from the MFC with plain MOF cathode (287.91 mW m−2).

From the potential curves of cathodes and anodes in Fig. 7b, it was observed that the anode potentials were similar, while the cathode potentials varied significantly. The measured open-circuit cathode potentials (vs. SHE) in MFCs followed the order: Pt/C (541 mV) > BGQDs/MOF-15 (470 mV) >BGQDs/MOF-10 (445 mV) > BGQDs/MOF-5 (444 mV) ＝FeCoMOF(444 mV) >BGQDs/MOF-20 (409 mV). The measured open-circuit cathode potential were noted almost ~300 mV lower than the theoretical cathode potential of 0.815 V at 25 °C, pH of 7 (Noori et al., 2019a). Generally, the potential differences between BGQDs/MOF-15 cathode and anode over the current range of 0.1 A m−2 to 1.6 A m−2 were the largest among all cases, which allowed higher power output as compared with other cases. The above result should be attributed to the coordination of BGQDs and FeCoMOF with unique morphology and abundant edges active sites, which could reduce charge transfer resistance, improve oxygen adsorption and the exposure of active sites.

Besides the activity, the long-term durability is another vital criterion for the practical feasibility of catalysts. Accordingly, the prolonged chronopotentiometry curves of NF, FeCoMOF, BGQDs/MOF-15 and Pt/C catalysts were performed at the potential of -0.2 V in O2 saturated PBS, as shown in Fig. 8 . After 50 h of operation, the current of BGQDs/MOF-15 cathode could maintain 91.2%, much higher than those of FeCoMOF (79.6%) and Pt/C (57.5%), while the NF exhibited extremely low stability. This result suggested that the BGQDs modification could improve the electrocatalytic stability of FeCoMOF. In MFCs, biofouling on membranes and cathodes is one of the critical issues, which would disintegrate the integrity of the cathode, decrease mass transfer and slow down cathode kinetics for ORR, and deteriorate the stability (Noori et al., 2019b). Biofouling mainly depends on the operating conditions of the MFC, such as the activities of microorganisms on the substrate, surface roughness and the duration of operation. To avoid biofouling, fabrication of antifouling membranes and the use of antibiofouling chemicals could be adopted (Ghasemi et al., 2013). In our case, the biofouling of the BGQDs/MOF-15 cathode could be negligible during our study period, because the cathodic chamber was fed with PBS, instead of wastewater. Fig. 8b reveals the excellent stability of BGQDs/MOF-15 in the MFC after 800 h, and the maximum voltage output maintained at about 0.6 V, while that from Pt/C electrode was 0.51 V and gradually declined after 350 h. The performance of the BGQDs/MOF-15 cathode catalyst in MFCs in terms of power density and stability was compared with those from recent literatures, as shown in Table S5. The BGQDs/MOF-15 showed excellent power density and stability.

HRTEM and XRD results showed that the morphology of BGQDs/MOF-15 was well maintained and there were no additional impurity peaks in the XRD pattern, indicating that no obvious crystal structure change occurred. Firstly, the in-situ growth of MOF on NF effectively enhanced the electrical conductivity and reduced the internal resistance. Secondly, BGQDs with superior conductivity and large number of edge active sites improved the reaction kinetics and promoted electron transport. Moreover, the strong M-O-C guaranteed excellent stability. These demonstrated the robust structure and good chemical stability of BGQDs/MOF-15 as ORR catalyst in MFC.

In summary, BGQDs implanted FeCoMOF in-situ grown on NF (BGQDs/MOF-t) hybrid catalyst was fabricated by a two-step hydrothermal and electrodeposition process. Surface modification of BGQDs could not only increase the surface functional groups and a large number of edge active sites, but also induced the synergistic effect between BGQDs and FeCoMOF, which is beneficial to improve the electrocatalytic activity towards ORR. The newly designed catalyst BGQDs/MOF-15 exhibited a remarkable electrocatalytic activity for ORR demonstrated by LSV, EIS, tafel tests, which was superior to those of FeCoMOF and commercial Pt/C. In MFC with BGQDs/MOF-15 cathode, the maximum power density of 703.55 mW m−2 was achieved, much higher than that from the MFC with Pt/C cathode. Furthermore, a prolonged stability of BGQDs/MOF-15 was obtained after BGQDs modification. The encouraging results provide a new avenue for developing cost-effective, highly efficient and robust hybrid catalysts for ORR and other electrochemical applications.
